sourceMovie.title: Casino
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Casino for the same reason it broke through in 1995: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a drama rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Martin Scorsese keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The crime and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: The Wolf of Wall Street
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Casino clicked for you, The Wolf of Wall Street (2013) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and drama, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: GoodFellas
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Casino clicked for you, GoodFellas (1990) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and drama, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Casino?
faq.answer: Try The Wolf of Wall Street, GoodFellas, Once Upon a Time in America—each shares DNA with Casino while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Casino?
faq.answer: Casino (1995) is best described as Crime, Drama. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Casino worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 8.0/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this crime lane.
